docs.title: Truly Local. Always On. Ready for Quests.
docs.text:
A party of specialized AI agents, running entirely on your machine, working through your tasks while you sleep.

No cloud. No subscriptions. No data leaving your machine. Ever.

docs.title: What is QuestChain?
docs.text:
QuestChain is an AI assistant that lives on your computer and actually does things — not just chats. It reads and writes your files, searches the web, runs commands, schedules jobs, and works through your task list autonomously in the background.

It runs on small, free, open-weight models through Ollama. No API keys, no monthly bill, no usage limits. Your hardware. Your data. Your agent.

docs.title: The vision
docs.text:
QuestChain runs a party of specialized micro-agents — each one built for a specific job. A researcher. A file keeper. A coder. A scheduler. They each have a name, a class, a set of tools, and a growing history of work.

Instead of one agent trying to do everything, each member of your party stays focused. That focus is what makes small local models productive — a dedicated researcher that only searches and reads sources will outperform a generalist every time.

Build your party. Level them up. Put them to work.

docs.title: What makes it different
docs.text:
It works offline. Disconnect your internet and QuestChain keeps running. The only thing that needs a connection is optional web search.

It never phones home. No telemetry. No analytics. No accounts. Optional API keys stay in a local file and go nowhere.

It works while you sleep. Drop a task into the quest folder and your agent picks it up automatically — on a timer, in the background, without you watching.

It grows with you. Every agent has a name, a class, a level, and a history. The more it works, the more capable it becomes. It's not a tool you reset — it's a companion you build.

It's open source. Free to use, fork, and modify. The self-coding loop is already live — QuestChain's own Builder agent has been writing features and pushing commits to the repo.

docs.title: Get started
docs.text:
questchain start --web

→ Install QuestChain

docs.title: Your Agents
docs.text:
QuestChain isn't one agent — it's a party. Each agent has a name, a class, a personality, and a set of tools matched to a specific kind of work.

You can create as many agents as you want, switch between them mid-conversation, and run different ones for different tasks.

docs.title: Agent classes
docs.text:
Class Best for Keeper 📚 Organizing files, taking notes, managing knowledge Explorer 🔭 Web research, finding information, summarizing sources Builder ⚒️ Writing and editing code, debugging, shipping features Planner 🔮 Planning projects, breaking down goals, strategic thinking Scheduler ⏱️ Setting up recurring tasks and automated jobs Custom 🌀 Whatever you want — you define the role and tools

Each class comes pre-configured with the right tools and a personality tuned for that domain. A Keeper knows not to run shell commands. An Explorer knows to verify sources before drawing conclusions. A Builder knows to delegate heavy coding to Claude Code.

docs.title: Why have multiple agents?
docs.text:
A focused agent makes better decisions. An agent that only does research doesn't have to think about file editing or scheduling — it just searches, reads, and synthesizes. That focus translates directly to better results, especially on smaller local models.

It also means you can build a team. Have a Keeper named Archivist who manages your notes, an Explorer named Scout who handles research, and a Builder named Jarvis who writes code. Switch between them with /agents.

docs.title: Creating an agent
docs.text:
Use /agents in the chat to open the agent manager. From there you can:

  • Create a new agent — give it a name, pick a class
  • Switch to a different agent
  • Edit an existing agent's name, class, or model

Each agent remembers its own conversation history and builds its own progression over time.

docs.title: Custom agents
docs.text: The Custom class is a blank slate. You decide what tools it has and how it behaves. Useful for highly specific workflows — a dedicated agent for one project, a client, or a niche task.

docs.title: Per-agent model override
docs.text: You can run different models on different agents. Run your Scheduler on a lightweight qwen3:1.7b to save resources, and your Builder on a more capable qwen3:8b for complex coding tasks. Set this when creating or editing an agent via /agents.

docs.title: What Can It Do?
docs.text: QuestChain is a general-purpose agent — if you can describe it, your agent can attempt it. Here's what it does well.

docs.title: Research & web search
docs.text:
Your Explorer agent can search the web and read full pages — not just headlines, but the actual content.

"Find the top 5 Python async libraries in 2026 and summarize what each one is best for. Save it to my notes."

"Look up the latest news on Qwen3 and give me a two-paragraph summary."

"Search for tutorials on FastAPI WebSocket authentication and pull out the key steps."

Powered by Tavily — set up in one command with /tavily.

docs.title: File management
docs.text:
Your Keeper agent can read, write, edit, search, and organize files on your real filesystem.

"Go through all the markdown files in my notes folder and create a summary document."

"Find every file in my project that imports requests and list them."

"Edit config.yaml — change the port from 3000 to 8080."

It reads files before editing them and confirms before anything destructive.

docs.title: Coding & development
docs.text:
Your Builder agent delegates programming work to Claude Code — Anthropic's coding agent — giving it full access to your filesystem to implement, test, and ship.

"Add input validation to the login form in auth.py."

"Refactor the database connection to use async/await."

"There's a bug where the search returns duplicate results — fix it."

QuestChain's own Builder agent has been writing its own features and pushing commits to the repo.

docs.title: Scheduled jobs
docs.text:
Your Scheduler agent can set up recurring tasks that run automatically and report back.

"Every morning at 8am, check the weather and send me a summary on Telegram."

"Run a backup of my project folder every night at midnight."

"Every Monday, search for the top AI news from the past week and save it to my notes."

Use /cron to see and manage all scheduled jobs.

docs.title: Background quests
docs.text:
Drop a task into a file and your agent picks it up automatically — no babysitting required.

Create a file: workspace/quests/summarize-inbox.md Contents: "Read all the files in workspace/inbox/ and write a one-paragraph summary of each."

The agent picks it up on the next check (every 60 minutes by default), completes it, and deletes the quest file. Results show up in the terminal and on Telegram if configured.

→ Learn more about Quests

docs.title: Voice output
docs.text: QuestChain can speak its responses aloud using local text-to-speech — no cloud required. Works in the terminal and sends voice messages on Telegram.

docs.title: Memory & personality
docs.text:
QuestChain remembers who you are across sessions. Run /onboard to teach it your name, what you work on, your preferences, and how you like to communicate. It stores this locally and uses it to personalize every response.

Use /memory to see exactly what it knows about you — and update it any time.

docs.title: Remote access via Telegram
docs.text:
Once set up, you can talk to your agent from your phone, anywhere — even when you're away from your machine. The same agent, same memory, same conversation.

→ Telegram Setup

docs.title: Starting QuestChain
docs.text:
questchain start --web                  # Start with web UI (recommended)
questchain start --web -m qwen3:4b      # Use a lighter model
questchain start --web --quests 30      # Check for quests every 30 minutes
questchain start --web --no-quests      # Disable background quests
questchain start -t <thread-id>         # Resume a specific conversation

Once running, open http://127.0.0.1:8765 in your browser.

docs.title: In-app commands
docs.text:
Type these directly in the chat at any time:

Command What it does /help Show all available commands /new Start a fresh conversation /agents Switch agents, create a new one, or edit existing ones /quest Open the quest manager — create, view, or delete quests /stats See your agent's level, XP, top tools, and achievements /memory View your saved user profile (what QuestChain knows about you) /model Show the current model and switch to a different one /tools List everything your agent can do /cron See scheduled jobs /instructions View your agent's current personality and rules /tavily Set up web search (free Tavily API key) /telegram Set up Telegram remote access /onboard Re-run the setup conversation /thread Show the current conversation ID Ctrl+D Exit

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
Switching agents mid-conversation: Use /agents to pick a different agent. Each one has its own focus, tools, and history.

Resuming a conversation: Every conversation has a thread ID (shown with /thread). Pass it with -t <id> at startup to pick up exactly where you left off.

Changing models on the fly: Use /model to see what's available and switch without restarting.

docs.title: Step 1: Install Ollama
docs.text:
Ollama runs the AI model on your machine. Download and install it from ollama.com/download, then start it:

ollama serve

Leave it running and open a new terminal for the next step.

docs.title: Step 2: Install QuestChain
docs.text:
macOS / LinuxWindows (PowerShell) 
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/RayP11/QuestChain/master/install.sh | bash

powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -c "irm https://raw.githubusercontent.com/RayP11/QuestChain/master/install.ps1 | iex"

That's it. The installer sets everything up automatically.

docs.title: Step 3: Run
docs.text:
questchain start --web

Open http://127.0.0.1:8765 in your browser and start chatting.

On first run, QuestChain walks you through a short setup — it learns your name, what you do, and how you like to work. After that, it remembers.

Enable web search (optional)

Type /tavily in the chat and paste in your free API key from tavily.com to give your agent live web search.

docs.title: Models
docs.text: QuestChain works with any model available in Ollama. These are the most tested.

docs.title: Tested models
docs.text:
Model VRAM Notes qwen3:8b ~6 GB Default. Fast, excellent tool calling, native thinking qwen3:4b ~3 GB Compact. Solid tool calling, native thinking qwen3.5:9b ~7 GB Strong tool calling across complex multi-step tasks qwen3.5:4b ~3.5 GB Lightweight, reliable tool calling qwen3:1.7b ~2 GB Ultra-light. Runs on CPU or minimal VRAM 
questchain start -m <any-model>   # use any model installed in Ollama

docs.title: Why small models work well here
docs.text:
Most agent frameworks are designed for large frontier models — they load every tool, every instruction, and every option into a single massive prompt. Small models struggle with that.

QuestChain does the opposite. Each agent is focused on a narrow role with only the tools it needs. Less noise means cleaner decisions. A 4B model that knows it's a researcher — and only has research tools — will consistently outperform a generalist model drowning in options.

QuestChain works great from 4B models and up. If you have more VRAM, larger models work even better — more capability, same focused approach. The framework scales with your hardware.

docs.title: Pulling a model
docs.text:
ollama pull qwen3:8b     # default
ollama pull qwen3:4b     # 3 GB VRAM
ollama pull qwen3:1.7b   # minimal hardware

Any model pulled into Ollama is immediately available to QuestChain via -m.

docs.title: No coding model?
docs.text: The Builder class delegates programming tasks to Claude Code via subprocess — so your local model doesn't need to be a coding specialist. If you don't have Claude Code, try deepseek-coder-v2:16b for maximum capability or qwen2.5-coder:7b for a lighter option.

docs.title: Progression
docs.text:
Your agent isn't a tool you reset. It's a companion you build.

Every agent starts at Level 1 and grows stronger through real work — the more it does, the more capable and experienced it becomes. Like a Pokémon, it evolves over time. Unlike a Pokémon, it's actually doing your tasks while you sleep.

docs.title: Leveling up
docs.text:
Every conversation, every web search, every file it edits, every quest it completes — it all counts. Your agent earns XP automatically just by being used. Hit enough XP and it levels up, all the way from Level 1 to Level 20.

The more specialized the work, the more it grows. A Builder that ships code levels up faster than one that just chats. An Explorer that digs deep across multiple sources earns more than one that skims.

docs.title: Achievements
docs.text:
Along the way, your agent earns achievements — permanent milestones that reflect what it's actually done.

First Strike · Awakening · Seasoned · Veteran · Legend · Century · Old Timer · Iron Will · Bibliophile · Web Walker · Globe Trotter · Blacksmith · Demolition · Archivist · Grand Planner · Polymath · Speed Demon · Centurion · Busy Bee · Road Runner

Each one tells a story. A Bibliophile has read hundreds of files. A Globe Trotter has browsed the web extensively. A Blacksmith has built real things.

docs.title: Check your agent's stats
docs.text:
Type /stats in the chat any time:

┌─ Jarvis (Builder ⚒️) ──────────────────────────────────┐
│ Level 7 · 3,240 XP                                      │
│ ████████████████░░░░░░░░░░░░ 64% to Level 8             │
│                                                          │
│ Top tools: claude_code (48×) · shell (31×) · read (27×) │
│ Achievements: First Strike · Blacksmith                  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

You can see exactly what your agent has been up to — what it reaches for most, how much ground it's covered, and what it's earned.

docs.title: Build your party
docs.text:
Create multiple agents, each with their own class, name, and progression. A high-level Explorer that's done thousands of searches. A Builder that's shipped real features. A Keeper that knows your entire notes archive inside and out.

The more you use them, the more they become yours.

docs.title: Quests
docs.text: Quests let your agent work in the background — autonomously, without you watching. Drop a task into a file, and your agent picks it up, completes it, and reports back.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
Every 60 minutes (configurable), QuestChain checks for pending quests, picks the first one, and gets to work. When it's done, the quest file is deleted and the result is shown in the chat — and sent to your phone if Telegram is set up.

You don't need to be at your computer. You don't need to watch it. It just works.

docs.title: Writing a quest
docs.text:
A quest is just a plain text file describing what you want done. Drop it in workspace/quests/ and your agent will find it.

workspace/quests/find-api-docs.md

Find the REST API docs for the weather service and save a summary to workspace/memory/weather-api.md

Write it like you'd write a note to a capable assistant. Be specific about what you want and where to save results.

docs.title: More quest examples
docs.text:
Research the top 5 project management tools in 2026.
Compare them on price, features, and offline support.
Save the comparison to workspace/memory/pm-tools.md

Go through all the files in workspace/inbox/ and write
a one-sentence summary of each. Save to workspace/memory/inbox-summary.md

Check the QuestChain GitHub repo for any new open issues
and summarize them in workspace/memory/issues.md

docs.title: Managing quests
docs.text: Type /quest in the chat to open the quest manager — create, view, and delete quests without leaving the app.

docs.title: Telegram Setup
docs.text: QuestChain runs alongside the CLI as a Telegram bot, giving you remote access from your phone. The same conversation thread and memory is shared between CLI and Telegram — switch between them mid-conversation.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
Run /telegram inside QuestChain and it walks you through the two-step wizard:

Step 1 — Get a bot token:

  1. Message @BotFather on Telegram
  2. Send /newbot and follow the prompts
  3. Copy the token it gives you

Step 2 — Get your user ID:

  1. Message @userinfobot on Telegram
  2. Copy your numeric user ID from the response

Paste both into the /telegram wizard. Credentials are saved automatically to ~/.questchain/.env.

docs.title: Starting with Telegram
docs.text:
Restart QuestChain after setup and the bot starts automatically alongside the CLI:

questchain start

No extra flags needed — if credentials are saved, the bot starts.

docs.title: Telegram commands
docs.text: Command Description /start Begin or resume a conversation /new Start a fresh conversation thread /quest Two-step wizard to create a new quest (title → content)

docs.title: Voice messages
docs.text:
If Kokoro TTS is configured, QuestChain sends voice messages on Telegram in addition to text — the same response delivered as audio.

Note

The Telegram bot only accepts messages from your user ID. Anyone else messaging the bot gets no response.
